title: Pointers & References
summary: Memory addresses, Java references vs C pointers, == vs equals, null safety, aliasing, and defensive copying.
guide:
# Pointers & References

## Pointer = Address of a Memory Location

A pointer is a variable that stores the **memory address** of another variable.
Java doesn't have explicit pointers, but it has 'references' — which are pointers
with safety rails (no arithmetic, no use-after-free).

Understanding pointers/references explains:
- Why two variables can affect each other's data
- Why null/undefined errors crash programs
- How linked lists and trees actually work
- Why Java is 'pass-by-value but objects feel like by-reference'
- The difference between == and .equals() in Java

---

## The Mental Model: Variables Are Boxes Holding Either Values or Addresses

```
PRIMITIVE (value type):
  int x = 42;

  Memory:
  ┌─────────────────┐
  │   x: 42         │   ← x's box holds the value 42 directly
  └─────────────────┘

REFERENCE (object):
  String s = "hello";

  Memory:
  ┌─────────────────┐         ┌─────────────────┐
  │   s: 0x7FA1     │ ──────► │  String 'hello' │  ← actual object on heap
  └─────────────────┘         └─────────────────┘
  (s holds an ADDRESS;  the object is somewhere else)

POINTER in C:
  int  x = 42;
  int* p = &x;       // p holds the address of x

  Memory:
  ┌─────────────────┐         ┌─────────────────┐
  │   p: 0x7FFC     │ ──────► │   x: 42         │
  └─────────────────┘         └─────────────────┘

  *p reads what's at the address (42). p reads the address itself.
```

---

## Java References vs C Pointers

```
+──────────────────────+──────────────────────+──────────────────────+
│ Feature              │ C Pointer            │ Java Reference       │
+──────────────────────+──────────────────────+──────────────────────+
│ Holds address?       │ Yes                  │ Yes                  │
│ Pointer arithmetic   │ Yes (p+1, p++)       │ NO                   │
│ Manual free          │ Yes (free(p))        │ NO (GC)              │
│ Can dangle           │ Yes (use-after-free) │ NO (GC keeps alive)  │
│ NULL pointer         │ Yes (dereferencing crashes)│ Yes (NullPointerException)│
│ Pointer-to-pointer   │ Yes (int**)          │ No                   │
│ Type safety          │ Loose (cast freely)  │ Strict (typed)       │
│ Function pointers    │ Yes                  │ Method references    │
+──────────────────────+──────────────────────+──────────────────────+

Java references = 'safe pointers'. You can:
  - dereference (call methods, access fields): obj.method()
  - reassign: obj = newObj;
  - compare with == (compares addresses, not contents!)
  - test for null: if (obj == null)

You CANNOT:
  - do address arithmetic
  - manually free
  - cast unrelated types
```

---

## == vs .equals() in Java

This is THE most common Java pitfall related to references.

```
==          compares ADDRESSES (do both refs point to same object?)
.equals()   compares CONTENTS (are the values logically equal?)

String s1 = "hello";
String s2 = "hello";
String s3 = new String("hello");

  s1 ──────────► [ String 'hello' ]   ← interned, both s1 and s2 point here
  s2 ─────────────────^
  s3 ──────────► [ String 'hello' ]   ← separate object via 'new'

  s1 == s2          → true   (same reference, due to string interning)
  s1 == s3          → false  (different objects on heap)
  s1.equals(s2)     → true   (same content)
  s1.equals(s3)     → true   (same content)

RULE: Always use .equals() for value comparison. == only for null check or identity.

FOR PRIMITIVES: == is correct (value comparison)
  int a = 5, b = 5;
  a == b → true

FOR Integer (boxed): subtle!
  Integer x = 100, y = 100;
  x == y → true (cached)
  Integer p = 200, q = 200;
  p == q → false (no cache for this range)
  Always use .equals() for Integer comparisons.
```

---

## Null References — The Billion Dollar Mistake

Tony Hoare, who invented null in 1965, called it his 'billion dollar mistake' for
the years of NullPointerException bugs it has caused.

```
String name = null;       // name points to nothing
name.length();            // NullPointerException at runtime!

// Why? You're trying to read from address 'nowhere'.
// In C: dereferencing NULL crashes the program.
// In Java: NPE thrown, can be caught.

DEFENCE STRATEGIES (modern Java):

1. Optional<T> — explicit absence
   Optional<User> findById(int id) { ... }
   findById(42).ifPresent(u -> println(u.getName()));

2. Check before access
   if (name != null) {
       println(name.length());
   }

3. Use Objects.requireNonNull() — fail fast at boundaries
   public void process(User u) {
       Objects.requireNonNull(u, "user must not be null");
       // continue with non-null u
   }

4. Annotations: @NonNull, @Nullable
   void greet(@NonNull String name) { ... }
   IDE/tools can warn at COMPILE time on null arguments.

5. Kotlin / Modern languages: nullable types in the type system
   String?  → may be null, must check
   String   → cannot be null, compiler enforces
```

---

## Aliasing — When Two Variables Point to Same Thing

```java
List<Integer> a = new ArrayList<>();
a.add(1);
a.add(2);

List<Integer> b = a;       // b points to SAME list
b.add(99);

System.out.println(a);     // [1, 2, 99]  — modified through b!
System.out.println(b);     // [1, 2, 99]
System.out.println(a == b); // true (same object)
```

**Visualisation:**

```
  Stack:                     Heap:
  ┌──────────────┐           ┌─────────────────────┐
  │ a: 0x100  ──┼───────────►│ ArrayList [1,2,99]  │
  │ b: 0x100  ──┼───────────►^                     │
  └──────────────┘           └─────────────────────┘

  Both a and b hold the same address. Mutations through either
  are visible to both. This is ALIASING.

  To get an independent copy:
  List<Integer> c = new ArrayList<>(a);  // copy constructor
  c.add(999);  // doesn't affect a or b
```

Aliasing causes subtle bugs in concurrent code, mutable shared state, and accidental
modifications. Defensive copy or immutable types prevent these.

---

## Linked Data Structures = Pointers in Action

```
A linked list is just nodes that hold references to each other:

  class Node {
      int value;
      Node next;       // reference to next node, or null
  }

  Node n3 = new Node(3, null);     // tail points to null
  Node n2 = new Node(2, n3);       // n2.next points to n3
  Node n1 = new Node(1, n2);       // n1.next points to n2

  Memory layout:

  Stack:                     Heap:
  n1 ────► [1 | next] ────► [2 | next] ────► [3 | null]

  Walk the list:
  Node curr = n1;
  while (curr != null) {
      System.out.println(curr.value);
      curr = curr.next;       // follow the reference
  }
  // Prints: 1, 2, 3

TREES, GRAPHS — same idea: each node holds references to others.
  Binary tree: node has left, right
  Graph: node has list<Node> neighbours
  All built on references / pointers.
```

---

## Shallow vs Deep Copy

When you copy an object that CONTAINS references, do you copy the references or the things they point to?

```java
class Person {
    String name;
    List<String> hobbies;
}

Person alice = new Person("Alice", new ArrayList<>(List.of("hiking")));

// Shallow copy: copy fields, but reference fields share same target
Person aliceCopy = new Person(alice.name, alice.hobbies);
aliceCopy.hobbies.add("reading");
// alice.hobbies now also contains 'reading'!
// because both point to same ArrayList

// Deep copy: recursively copy all referenced objects
Person aliceDeep = new Person(alice.name, new ArrayList<>(alice.hobbies));
aliceDeep.hobbies.add("swimming");
// alice.hobbies unchanged — independent list

// In Java, default Object.clone() is shallow.
// For deep copy: implement copy constructor or use serialisation/Cloneable + override.
```

**Visualisation:**

```
Original (alice):
  Person ──► name: 'Alice'
         └─► hobbies ──► [hiking]

Shallow copy:
  PersonA ──► name: 'Alice'           ← copied (or shared if String pool)
          └─► hobbies ──┐
  PersonB ──► name: 'Alice'
          └─► hobbies ──┴─► [hiking]    ← BOTH point to same list!

Deep copy:
  PersonA ──► name: 'Alice'
          └─► hobbies ──► [hiking]      ← original list
  PersonB ──► name: 'Alice'
          └─► hobbies ──► [hiking]      ← INDEPENDENT copy
```

---

## C Pointer Examples (Just for Mental Model)

```c
// Basic pointer
int x = 10;
int* p = &x;       // p = address of x
*p = 20;           // dereference: write 20 to where p points
// Now x == 20

// Pointer arithmetic (Java has nothing like this)
int arr[] = {1, 2, 3, 4};
int* p = arr;      // p points to arr[0]
*(p + 2);          // *(p+2) = arr[2] = 3
p++;               // now p points to arr[1]

// Pointer to pointer
int x = 5;
int* p = &x;
int** pp = &p;     // pp = address of p
**pp = 99;         // dereference twice: x = 99

// Common bug: dangling pointer
int* p;
{
    int x = 10;
    p = &x;
}  // x goes out of scope; p now dangles!
*p; // undefined behaviour — x is gone

// Java: GC keeps x alive as long as p references it. No dangling possible.
```

---

## Mind Map

```
POINTERS & REFERENCES
|
+-- WHAT THEY ARE
|   +-- Variable holding a memory address
|   +-- Java references = safe pointers
|   +-- C pointers = unrestricted
|
+-- JAVA SPECIFICS
|   +-- == compares addresses
|   +-- .equals() compares contents
|   +-- Always .equals() for objects
|   +-- Integer auto-unbox subtleties
|
+-- NULL
|   +-- Address pointing to nothing
|   +-- Dereferencing → NullPointerException
|   +-- Defences: Optional, requireNonNull, @NonNull
|
+-- ALIASING
|   +-- Two refs to same object
|   +-- Mutations through one visible to other
|   +-- Defensive copy or immutability prevents bugs
|
+-- SHALLOW vs DEEP COPY
|   +-- Shallow: copies references, shares targets
|   +-- Deep: recursively copies everything
|
+-- DATA STRUCTURES
    +-- Linked list = nodes with next references
    +-- Tree = node with left, right references
    +-- Graph = node with list<Node>
    +-- All built on references
```

---

## How Pointers/References Connect to Other Topics

- **Memory (stack/heap)**: references on stack point to heap objects
- **Linked List, Tree, Graph**: nodes connected by references
- **Garbage Collection**: GC tracks references to determine liveness
- **Concurrency**: shared mutable state via aliasing causes races
- **Immutability**: prevents aliasing bugs by design (Strings, records)

---

## Common Beginner Mistakes

1. **Using == for String/object equality** — compares addresses, not content
2. **NullPointerException without checking** — always validate or use Optional
3. **Sharing mutable state** without realising it's the same object (aliasing)
4. **Shallow copy when deep copy needed** — modifications leak across copies
5. **Modifying a list while iterating** — ConcurrentModificationException
6. **Returning a reference to internal state** — caller can mutate your private data

---

## References

### Videos
- **Pointers in C/C++ Explained** by mycodeschool: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=LMoyzKduFc4
- **Java Pass-By-Value vs Reference** by Cave of Programming: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=t7SBOSmmMIA

### Articles
- **Pass-by-value in Java** (Baeldung): https://www.baeldung.com/java-pass-by-value-or-pass-by-reference
- **Optional in Java** (Baeldung): https://www.baeldung.com/java-optional

questions.prompt: What does a pointer/reference variable actually store?
choices: The MEMORY ADDRESS of another variable or object — not the value itself
questions.explanation: Pointers/references hold an address. Through that address, you can access the actual data. The variable holding the address is small (typically 8 bytes); the object it points to may be much larger and lives elsewhere (heap).

questions.prompt: In Java, what does '==' compare for object references?
choices: The ADDRESS — whether both variables point to the SAME object on the heap. Use .equals() for content comparison.
questions.explanation: == compares addresses for objects. s1 == s2 is true only if both refer to identical heap object. For value comparison: .equals(). 'String a = new String("x"); String b = new String("x"); a == b → false. a.equals(b) → true'. This trips up beginners constantly.

choices: false, false — they're different objects with same content
questions.explanation: s1 = literal → interned in String pool. s2 = new String() → new heap object. Different addresses (==) gives false. Same content, .equals() gives true. ALWAYS use .equals() for String comparison. == only works coincidentally with interned literals.

questions.code: aliasing — two refs to same list
choices: [1, 2, 99] — a and b are aliases (same object); modification through b is visible through a
questions.explanation: List<Integer> b = a does NOT copy. b becomes another reference to the same ArrayList. b.add(99) modifies the shared object. a sees the change. To get an independent copy: 'new ArrayList<>(a)'. Aliasing is a leading source of bugs in mutable shared state.

questions.prompt: What is a NullPointerException in Java?
choices: Runtime error when you dereference (call method on / access field of) a reference that is null — pointing to no object
questions.explanation: NPE: you tried to use a null reference. 'String s = null; s.length();' throws NPE. The JVM checks before each method call/field access. Defences: Optional<T>, null checks, @NonNull annotations, requireNonNull at boundaries. Most common Java bug in production.

questions.prompt: What is the difference between shallow copy and deep copy?
choices: Shallow: copies fields (reference fields point to same target). Deep: recursively copies all referenced objects, creating fully independent structure.
questions.explanation: Shallow copy: a Person with a List field — copy gets new Person but same List. Modifying list via either Person affects both. Deep copy: recursively allocates new List, new contents. Independent. Default Object.clone() is shallow. Use copy constructors for deep.

questions.prompt: In Java, calling 'list.add(99)' inside a method DOES modify the caller's list. Why?
choices: Java passes references by value: the address bits are copied, but both the local copy and caller's variable point to the SAME list on the heap. Mutating through the reference is visible to caller.
questions.explanation: Java is always pass-by-value. For objects, the value passed is the reference (address). Method's local var has its own copy of the address but it points to the same object. Mutating fields is visible. Reassigning the local var (list = new ArrayList<>()) is NOT visible to caller.

choices: true, false — Integer caches values -128 to 127, so a and b share, but c and d are new objects
questions.explanation: Integer.valueOf() caches -128 to 127 (auto-boxing range). Within range: same reference, == works. Outside range: new objects, == fails. CONCLUSION: never use == for Integer/Long etc. Always .equals() or unbox to int first. This is a notorious Java pitfall.

questions.prompt: What is a 'dangling pointer' in C?
choices: A pointer to memory that has been freed/deallocated. Reading or writing through it is undefined behaviour — may crash, may silently corrupt.
questions.explanation: Dangling pointer in C: free(p) but still using p. Memory may be reused for something else. Writing to it corrupts whatever now lives there. Java: GC keeps objects alive as long as references exist — no dangling possible. C++ smart pointers (unique_ptr, shared_ptr) prevent these bugs.

questions.prompt: What does Optional<T> in Java solve?
choices: Makes the absence of a value EXPLICIT in the type signature, forcing the caller to handle it. Reduces NullPointerException by design.
questions.explanation: Optional<User> findById(int id): caller knows the result MAY be absent. Compiler reminds them: must call .orElse(), .ifPresent(), .map() etc. Better than nullable User which silently allows NPE. Best practice: never return null from public APIs — use Optional.

questions.prompt: What is the difference between Java references and C pointers?
choices: Java refs are 'safe pointers': no arithmetic (p+1), no manual free, can't dangle (GC), strict types. C pointers allow all of these but with greater bug risk.
questions.explanation: Java refs are restricted: no &p, no *p, no p++, no p+5. The JVM/GC manage memory. Strict typing prevents casting unrelated types. C is more powerful (raw memory access) but error-prone. Modern C++ smart pointers add safety similar to Java refs.

questions.prompt: In linked list traversal: 'while (curr != null) { ... curr = curr.next; }' — what is happening?
choices: Following references: curr starts at head, then re-points to each next node via the curr.next reference field. When curr = null, end of list reached.
questions.explanation: Linked list = chain of nodes connected by 'next' references. Traversal: walk references one at a time. Each node's 'next' field is a reference to the next node, or null if it's the last. This is the canonical use of pointers/references for data structures.

questions.prompt: You return your private List<Integer> from a getter. What's the bug?
choices: Caller has a reference to your INTERNAL list and can mutate it. Encapsulation broken. Fix: return new ArrayList<>(internal) or Collections.unmodifiableList(internal).
questions.explanation: Returning the same reference exposes internal state. Caller does .add(...) and corrupts your object. Defensive copy: return new ArrayList<>(internal). Or unmodifiable view: Collections.unmodifiableList(internal). Or use immutable collection (List.copyOf). Common encapsulation bug.

questions.prompt: Why is Java's String class immutable?
choices: Safety: many things hold references to a String (HashMap keys, security checks). If String were mutable, holders could see the value change unexpectedly. Immutability eliminates aliasing risks.
questions.explanation: Immutable String prevents aliasing bugs: holder can trust the value won't change. HashMap rebuilds wouldn't be needed (key contents stable). Concurrency: no synchronisation. Security: pathnames, class names can't be mutated mid-check. Immutability is a design pattern that eliminates whole classes of bugs.

questions.prompt: Which prevent NullPointerException? (Select all)
choices: Adding more null checks everywhere defensively
questions.explanation: All three actually help. Optional makes absence explicit in the type. requireNonNull fails fast at boundaries. @NonNull lets tools warn at compile. Defensive null checks 'everywhere' (option 4) is an anti-pattern — bloats code, hides intent. Better: design with non-nullable defaults, use Optional only where absence is meaningful.

questions.prompt: What is 'aliasing' in programming?
choices: Two or more variables referencing the SAME underlying object — modifications through one are visible through all others
questions.explanation: Aliasing: 'b = a' for objects creates an alias. Both vars point to same heap object. Modifying through b affects view from a. Causes bugs especially in mutable shared state. Mitigations: defensive copy, immutability, ownership types (Rust). Source of many concurrency bugs.

questions.prompt: In Java, 'a.equals(b)' returns true but 'a.hashCode() != b.hashCode()'. What's the bug?
choices: CONTRACT violation: if a.equals(b) is true, hashCode() MUST be equal. Otherwise HashMap and HashSet break (two equal keys land in different buckets, can't be retrieved).
questions.explanation: Java's equals/hashCode contract: equal objects MUST have equal hash codes. If you override equals(), you MUST override hashCode() consistently. Otherwise HashMap.get(key) won't find your object: it computes hash, goes to bucket B, but your equal-key was placed in bucket A.

questions.prompt: What is a memory address in plain terms?
choices: A unique number identifying one specific location in RAM. Like a postal address for a building.
questions.explanation: RAM is a giant array. Each byte has a unique address (e.g. 0x7FFC000A in 64-bit). Pointer/reference = a number representing this address. To read what's there: dereference the pointer. The OS and CPU translate virtual addresses to physical addresses (you don't see physical).

questions.prompt: In Kotlin, 'String' vs 'String?' — what's the difference?
choices: String CANNOT be null (compiler enforces). String? CAN be null and you must explicitly handle it (?. or !!). Type system encodes nullability.
questions.explanation: Kotlin uses non-null types by default. 'val s: String' compiler ensures s is never null. 'val s: String?' requires safe calls (s?.length) or assertions (s!!.length). Eliminates NullPointerException at compile time. Java is gradually adding similar with @NonNull/@Nullable but not enforced by language.

questions.prompt: Why does this defensive copy NOT fully protect: 'return new ArrayList<>(internalList)' for List<Person>?
choices: Shallow copy only — the new list contains the SAME Person references. Caller can still mutate Person objects. Need DEEP copy: stream and copy each Person.
questions.explanation: new ArrayList<>(other) creates a new list but shares element references. If Person is mutable, caller can do list.get(0).setName('attacker'). To fully protect: return new ArrayList containing copies of each Person. Best: make Person immutable (record + final fields).

flashcards.back: Variable holding a memory address. Through it you access the actual data. Java refs = safe pointers (no arithmetic, no manual free, no dangling — GC tracks). C pointers = full power but error-prone.

== compares ADDRESSES (same object?). .equals() compares CONTENTS (same value?). For Strings/Objects: ALWAYS .equals(). For primitives (int, char, boolean): == works. Integer caches -128..127 — never use == for boxed types.

flashcards.front: Aliasing and how to prevent bugs
flashcards.back: Aliasing: 'b = a' makes b another reference to same object. Mutation through b visible to a. Defences: 1) Immutable types (record, String). 2) Defensive copy (new ArrayList<>(...)). 3) Unmodifiable views (Collections.unmodifiableList).

1) Optional<T> for return values. 2) Objects.requireNonNull(x, msg) at method entry. 3) @NonNull annotations + tooling. 4) Kotlin-style nullable types in modern languages. AVOID excessive null-checking everywhere.

Shallow: copy fields, reference fields share targets. Deep: recursively copy referenced objects too. Object.clone() default = shallow. For deep: copy constructor with new collections + cloned elements. Safer: immutable types eliminate need.

Java is ALWAYS pass-by-value. For primitives: value copied. For objects: REFERENCE value (address) copied. Both refs point to same heap object — mutations visible. Re-binding the local ref does NOT propagate to caller. There's no pass-by-reference in Java.

Linked list: Node{value, Node next}. Tree: Node{left, right}. Graph: Node{List<Node> neighbours}. Walk: while(curr!=null) { use curr; curr=curr.next; }. Build: new Node(value, existingNode). All built on references.

If a.equals(b), then a.hashCode() == b.hashCode(). Override BOTH together. Violation breaks HashMap, HashSet (key in wrong bucket, can't be retrieved). IDE generates both. Use records (auto-generates correctly) or Objects.hash() helper.

project.brief: You're reviewing a junior dev's Java code. They've created a User class with: a password field (String), a List<Role> roles, and methods for getRoles() returning the list directly, equals() based only on user.id, and missing hashCode(). Identify all reference/pointer-related issues, explain the implications, and rewrite the class safely.

checklist.text: getRoles() exposes internal list — caller can mutate User's roles. Fix: return Collections.unmodifiableList(roles) or new ArrayList<>(roles), or return immutable List.copyOf(roles).
hashCode() missing while equals() overridden — breaks HashMap/HashSet contract. Two equal users might land in different buckets. Add hashCode() returning Objects.hash(id) consistent with equals.
Password as String — JVM keeps Strings in pool, can't securely wipe. Use char[] (clearable with Arrays.fill) or modern: java.security.Password. Defensive copy on input.
Constructor takes List<Role> — store defensive copy: this.roles = new ArrayList<>(roles). Otherwise external mutations of caller's list affect User's state.
Better: convert to a record (Java 14+): User(int id, char[] password, List<Role> roles). Records auto-generate equals/hashCode/toString correctly. Combine with List.copyOf in compact constructor.

hints: Records solve most of these issues automatically: equals/hashCode generated from all components, fields are final (no aliasing leak via setters).
If User must be a class (not record): minimum: defensive copy in constructor, unmodifiable getter, both equals AND hashCode using id only, and char[] for password.
Modern Java: 'Collection.unmodifiableList' returns a view (caller can't mutate) but if you mutate internal, they see it. 'List.copyOf' returns truly independent immutable copy.
